coredata.dc:title: Design and fabrication of fluorescence resonance energy transfer-mediated fluorescent polymer nanoparticles for ratiometric sensing of lysosomal pH
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jian
Tang, Ying
Wang, Hong
Zhang, Peisheng
Li, Ya
Jiang, Jianhui
coredata.dc:description: The design of effective tools capable of sensing lysosome pH is highly desirable for better understanding its biological functions in cellular behaviors and various diseases. Herein, a lysosome-targetable ratiometric fluorescent polymer nanoparticle pH sensor (RFPNS) was synthesized via incorporation of miniemulsion polymerization and surface modification technique. In this system, the donor: 4-ethoxy-9-allyl-1,8-naphthalimide (EANI) and the acceptor: fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) were covalently linked to the polymer nanoparticle to construct pH-responsive fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system. The FITC moieties on the surface of RFPNS underwent structural and spectral transformation as the presence of pH changes, resulting in ratiometric fluorescent sensing of pH. The as-prepared RFPNS displayed favorable water dispersibility, good pH-induced spectral reversibility and so on. Following the living cell uptake, the as-prepared RFPNS with good cell-membrane permeability can mainly stain in the lysosomes; and it can facilitate visualization of the intracellular lysosomal pH changes. This nanosensor platform offers a novel method for future development of ratiometric fluorescent probes for targeting other analytes, like ions, metabolites,and other biomolecules in biosamples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer nanoparticle
FRET
Ratiometric
Lysosomal pH

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of pH in buffer solutions
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of pH in buffer solutions
Lysosome-targetable fluorescence imaging in living cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM image of a typical RFPNS sample (NP-R2).

RFPNS samples with donor–acceptor weight ratios of (A) 0.2, (B) 0.3 and (C) 0.4, respectively. (D) The ratiometric fluorescence intensity changes (on/off at 432nm and 528nm, i.e. I(432, pH 2.0)/I(432, pH 8.0) and I(528, pH 8.0)/I(528, pH 2.0)) (λex =385nm).

(A) Fluorescence photograph of the typical NP-R2 (0.78mg/mL) at different pH (2.0 and 8.0) levels under a 365nm ultraviolet irradiation. (B) Fluorescence decay curves of representative NP-R2 (0.78mg/mL) at different pH levels (2.0 and 8.0). The data was collected at 432nm and λex =385nm.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of typical NP-R2 (0.78mg/mL) in different pH buffer solutions (2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5 and 8.0); (B) The ratio of I 528/I 432 versus pH value, as well as the corresponding linear fitting curve.

Confocal fluorescence images for live HeLa cells co-stained with RFPNS (0.05mg/mL) and Lyso Tracker Red (500nM) or MitoTracker Red (500nM). The fluorescence images in blue channel were collected at 420–490nm (A and D, for RFPNS) and green channel were collected at 575–675nm (B or E, for Lyso Tracker Red or MitoTracker Red) with the excitation wavelength 405nm and 559nm, respectively. (C) and (F): overlay images. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(I) Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of RFPNS (0.05mg/mL) in HeLa cells exposed to different pH media. The images of the first column (A, F, K) exhibit the corresponding bright field images. The second (B, G, L) and third (C, H, M) column were collected in blue channel (420–490nm) and green channel (500–560nm) with the excitation wavelength 405nm, respectively. The forth column (D, I, N) exhibits the overlay of green channel and blue channel. The images of the five columns (the ratio channel) were acquired by Image pro software. The color strip on the right shows the pseudo color change with pH. (II): Quantification of fluorescence intensities for group (E), (J) and (O), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(I) Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of RFPNS (0.05mg/mL) in HeLa cells treatment with bafilomycin A1 for 30min before and after. The images of the first column (A, F) show the bright field images. The second (B, G) and third (C, H) column were collected in blue channel (420–490nm) and green channel (500–560nm) with the excitation wavelength 405nm, respectively. The forth column (D, I) exhibits the overlay of green channel and blue channel. The images of the five columns (the ratio channel) were acquired by Image pro software. The color strip on the right shows the pseudo color change with pH. (II): Quantification of fluorescence intensities for group (E) and (J), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of preparation of RFPNS and their application as ratiometric fluorescent sensor for pH in water.
List of some data and parameters of various BRFPNS samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated by using the absorbance of EANI at 366nm in nanoparticle dispersion and the molar extinction coefficient of EANI in dichloromethane (ε=13,000mol−1 Lcm−1).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated by using the absorbance of FITC at 490nm on the surface of nanoparticles and the molar extinction coefficient of FITC in water (ε=74,152mol−1 Lcm−1).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average diameter of nanoparticles were determined from DLS data.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Size distribution of nanoparticles were determined by DLS.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average nanoparticle diameter, determined from DLS data.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The average number of EANI in a nanoparticle.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The ratio of the average number of FITC and EANI in a nanoparticle.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The experimental energy transfer efficiency at pH 8.

head.ce:title: Design and fabrication of fluorescence resonance energy transfer-mediated fluorescent polymer nanoparticles for ratiometric sensing of lysosomal pH

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education, Hunan Province College Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, Hunan 411201, PR China

Hunan Province College Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China (J. Chen).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A lysosome-targetable ratiometric fluorescent polymer nanoparticle pH sensor (RFPNS) was synthesized via incorporation of miniemulsion polymerization and surface modification technique. The as-prepared RFPNS displayed favorable water dispersibility, good pH-induced spectral reversibility and so on. Following the living cell uptake, the as-prepared RFPNS with good cell-membrane permeability can mainly stain in the lysosomes; and it can facilitate visualization of the intracellular lysosomal pH changes.

The design of effective tools capable of sensing lysosome pH is highly desirable for better understanding its biological functions in cellular behaviors and various diseases. Herein, a lysosome-targetable ratiometric fluorescent polymer nanoparticle pH sensor (RFPNS) was synthesized via incorporation of miniemulsion polymerization and surface modification technique. In this system, the donor: 4-ethoxy-9-allyl-1,8-naphthalimide (EANI) and the acceptor: fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) were covalently linked to the polymer nanoparticle to construct pH-responsive fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system. The FITC moieties on the surface of RFPNS underwent structural and spectral transformation as the presence of pH changes, resulting in ratiometric fluorescent sensing of pH. The as-prepared RFPNS displayed favorable water dispersibility, good pH-induced spectral reversibility and so on. Following the living cell uptake, the as-prepared RFPNS with good cell-membrane permeability can mainly stain in the lysosomes; and it can facilitate visualization of the intracellular lysosomal pH changes. This nanosensor platform offers a novel method for future development of ratiometric fluorescent probes for targeting other analytes, like ions, metabolites,and other biomolecules in biosamples.

ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular acidic pH plays a pivotal role in many cellular metabolic pathways, including endocytic processes, signaling, apoptosis, and defense [1]. Lysosomes, as acidic organelles, degrade macromolecules and cell components by means of interior hydrolytic enzymes [2]. Undoubtedly, the pH oflysosomes is critical for endocytosis, autophagy and so on [3]. However, acatastatic of the lysosomal pH is known to be associated with various pathological cellular dysfunctions, which can induce an increased risk of many diseases such as cancer, neurodegenerative disorders, Alzheimer’s disease [4]. To better understand the physiological and pathological processes closely relevant to lysosomal pH, thus, development of efficient chemosensors for monitoring lysosomal pH changes is of vital importance.

In recent years, fluorescent sensors have emerged as robust tools for detecting various analytes, due to their ease of use, high sensitivity, high selectivity, excellent temporal-spatial resolution and fairly simple technical implementation [5–11]. A number of fluorescent sensors based on one intensity changes (“turn-on” or “turn-off”) have been reported [12–20]. However, many of the fluorescent sensors have only one emission signal for detecting. Hence, the sensor concentration, photobleaching, excitation intensity and the environmental and instrumental conditions will affect the detection result [11]. Ratiometric fluorescent sensors based on two emission signals at two distinct wavelengths can effectively eliminate or reduce the effects of these factors [11].

Recently, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) has been employed to design ratiometric sensors [21–28]. For example, Hamachi and co-workers developed a ratiometric polyphosphate probe based on binding-induced modulation of FRET between coumarin and xanthene skeleton, the probe has been used for real-time fluorescence monitoring of enzyme reactions and ratiometric visualization of ATP in living cells [21]. Tian and co-workers reported a FRET-based ratiometric pH sensor for visualizing stimulus-responsive changes of intracellular pH values through integrating an amino-naphthalimide pH probe as an FRET acceptor with a pH-insensitive coumarin fluorophore as an FRET donor [22]. To date, several FRET-based ratiometric fluorescent chemosensors for lysosomal pH have been reported [29–33]. However, all of these sensors are small molecules, which suffer from less satisfactory water solubility, can only operate in organic or mixed aqueous organic solvents, and these sensors easily aggregate in water due to the intermolecular π-π interactions, as noted in recent articles by Lippard and Nolan [34] and Smet et al. [35]. Thus, the design of water-soluble (or dispersible) FRET-based ratiometric chemosensors for lysosomal pH is promising and of high practical value.

It should be also noted that the real-time monitoring of analytes (ions, metabolites andbiomolecules) in vivo using small molecule chemosensors is restricted by inherent limitations such as instant elimination and extravasation out of the vasculature [36,37]. To overcome these limitations, polymer nanoparticles, with covalent incorporation of analyte-responsive receptor, have been developed to the preparation of FRET-based ratiometric fluorescent sensing systems [38]. It is known that the as-prepared polymer nanoparticles exhibit not only good water solubility, large accumulation capacity, long in vivo circulation times, but also facile preparation, flexible design, and tunable structure and function [36,37]. Specially, the commercially available methacrylate monomers provide a simple, low cost, and broadly accessible approach for preparing highly stabilized polymer nanoparticles [39]. So far, several polymer nanoparticle-based FRET ratiometric chemosensors specific for pH have been created [23,24,40–42]. Despite these efforts, to our best knowledge, polymer nanoparticle-based FRET ratiometric chemosensor capable of sensing lysosomal pH changes is still rare. Thus, it is highly desirable to construct polymer nanoparticle-based FRET systems for ratiometric sensing lysosomal pH changes.

In this work, we report on the fabrication of ratiometric fluorescent polymer nanoparticle sensor (RFPNS) for monitoring lysosomal pH changes via combination of miniemulsion polymerization [43] and surface modification technique. FRET pair consisting of 4-ethoxy-9-allyl-1,8-naphthalimide (EANI) [44] donor and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC, acceptor) with pH response characteristics. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , with increasing of pH, FITC turns into ring-opened form. The FRET process can be taken place between the donor (EANI) in the particle cores and the surface acceptor (ring-opened fluorescein), achieving the ratiometric fluorescence recognition of pH in aqueous media. Moreover, with the help of laser scanning confocal microscope, the as-prepared BRFPNS were proven to selectively accumulate in the lysosomes of living cells, and the ratiometric imaging of the intracellular lysosomal pH was successfully monitored based on this new sensing platform.

The surfactant cetyltrimethyl ammonium chloride (CTAC, 99.0%), n-hexadecane (HD, 99%), 2-aminoethyl methacrylate hydrochloride (AEMH 90%), fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC 96%) were used as received from Sigma-Aldrich. The phenolic inhibitor in methyl methacrylate (MMA, Aldrich) was removed by washing three times with 10% sodium hydroxide solution and deionized water, and then purified by vacuum distillation. Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN, 99.99%, Aldrich) was recrystallized from ethanol and dried under vacuum. The double-distilled water used in this work was further purified with a Milli-Q system. Triethylamine (A.R.) was distilled over CaH2. Other reagents were analytical reagents and used without further purification.

A mixture containing the monomer (MMA, 0.5g) hydrophobic dyes (EANI see Table 1 ), polymerization initiator (AIBN, 0.025g), and hydrophobe (HD, 0.075g) was added to 10mL water solution with emulsifier (CTAC, 0.1g) and AEMH (0.08g) and stirred (1000r/min) for 10min, then, the mixture was ultrasonicated for 15min (JY92-IIN) to obtain a stable miniemulsion. The mixture was cooled in an ice-bath during ultrasonication to avoid overheated. The resulting miniemulsion was put into a 50mL flask equipped with a condenser, which was immersed in an oil bath with a thermostat. The miniemulsion was polymerized at 75°C for 3h.

Subsequently, the as-prepared nanoparticles dispersion was transferred into a 25mL round bottom flask. After the pH value of the dispersion was adjusted to ca. 7.5, 5mg of FITC was added to the dispersion. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 2days. The excess of FITC was removed by dialysis through a porous cellulose membrane (MWCO 3500) toward an aqueous solution of CTAC (10wt%). A stable RFPNS dispersion was obtained (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of pH in buffer solutions
ce:section.ce:para: A series of standard pH buffers (Britton-Robison (BR), H3PO4-HAc-H3BO3) was prepared with 40mM H3PO4, HAc and H3BO3 adjusted by NaOH (200mM). In a 5mL cuvette, 30μL of 78mg/mL BRFPNS (final concentration, 0.78mg/mL) dispersion was diluted by adding appropriate volume of buffer solutions ranging from pH 2–8. The final volume of liquid in the cuvette was adjusted to 3.0mL, and the resulting solutions were transferred into quartz cells to measure the fluorescence spectra (λex =385nm). The fluorescence spectra were recorded at varied pH values and the fluorescence ratios (R=I528/I432) were calculated from the fluorescence intensities at 528 and 432nm.

The effect of RFPNS on cell proliferation in HeLa cells was performed via the CellTiter 96 AQueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay. First, cells were seeded at a density of 5.0×103 cells per well in 96-well plates. Then, cells were dealt with various diluted samples of RFPNS in the presence of 10% serum. After 24h of incubation, 96-well plates were washed with PBS buffer for three times, and incubated for another 1h at 37°C with fresh medium containing CellTiter 96 AQueous One Solution. Thereafter, the absorbance of each plate was measured via a Thermo Scientific Multiskan MK3Microplate Reader (Thermo Fisher, USA) at 492nm.

For the co-localization imaging, the cells were stained firstly with the fresh cell growth medium supplemented with RFPNS (0.05mg/mL) for 3h, then Lyso Tracker Red (500nM) or Mito-Tracker red (500nM) in cell culture medium was added to pre-washed cells and incubated at 37°C for 30min. After removing the culture medium, the cells were washed with PBS and imaged on an Olympus FV1000 confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscope containing an Olympus IX81 inverted microscope. Channel 1 (RFPNS): excitation: 405nm, blue emission collected: 420–490nm, green emission collected: 500–560nm; Channel 2 (Lyso Tracker or Mito-Tracker): excitation: 559nm, emission collected: 575–675nm.

For the intracellular pH imaging, HeLa cells were seeded in confocal dishes at a density of ca. 20% per plate and cultured for 24h at 37°C. Then, 500μL of fresh cell growth medium supplemented with RFPNS (0.05mg/mL) was added to each dish. After incubation for 3h, the dishes were washed with PBS for three times and incubated for another 10min with PBS (pH 7.4), acetic acid in PBS (pH 4.7) and 20mM NH4Cl in PBS, respectively. Then, HeLa cells were incubated with 1mL of fresh medium for fluorescence imaging. All fluorescence images were acquired using an oil immersion objective (100×, NA 1.3) on an Olympus FV1000 confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscope containing an Olympus IX81 inverted microscope. Excitation: 405nm, blue emission collected: 420–490nm, green emission collected: 500–560nm.

On the other hand, to block the maturation of lysosomes, cells were pre-incubated with 1μM bafilomycin A1 in fresh cell growth medium for 30min. Then, 500μL of fresh cell growth medium supplemented with RFPNS (0.05mg/mL) was added to each dish. After incubation for 3h, the dishes were washed with PBS and imaged on an Olympus FV1000 confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscope containing an Olympus IX81 inverted microscope. Excitation: 405nm, blue emission collected: 420–490nm, green emission collected: 500–560nm.

The 1H NMR spectra was measured on a Bruker Avance-II 500MHz NMR spectrometer. The nanoparticle diameters were determined by a Malvern Nano-ZS90 instrument and their morphology was observed with an atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon) in the tapping mode and a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL USA, Inc.). UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2501PC spectrophotometer at room temperature (298K). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature (298K). Fluorescence lifetime (τ) measurements were carried out with a time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) nanosecond fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS920) at ambient temperature (298K). The pH values were measured by a Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd pHS-25 pH-meter.

In current study, well-defined RFPNS was fabricated via incorporating a facile one-pot miniemulsion polymerization and surface modification method, as shown in Scheme 1. As the presence of an unsaturated polymerizable double bond in EANI dye, it is easily copolymerized with vinyl monomers to form a covalent bond in the polymer matrix [44]. In addition, FITC can be easily conjugated onto the surface of the nanoparticles through the reaction between the amino groups on the surfaces of nanoparticles and the isothiocyanate groups in FITC [23]. Herein, the covalent incorporation of dyes was demonstrated by 1H NMR (Fig. S1) and absorption spectra (Fig. S2). It can effectively prevent the leakage of dye, and ensure stable FRET efficiency between donor and acceptor. The as-prepared RFPNS with an EANI fluorescent core and FITC shell exhibit FRET-based pH-sensitive properties in 100% aqueous media due to the structure transformation of FITC under acidic or alkaline situation [36].

It is well-known that the FRET-based system with smaller nanoparticles is more grateful for the accurate ratiometric detection [45]. In this study, the RFPNS with the uniform diameter from 58 to 67nm, as measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS, see Table 1), were achieved via modulating some experimental parameters.

Moreover, as depicted in Table 1, the PDI of the as-prepared nanoparticle samples are not varied before and after linking of FITC. Fig. 1 exhibits an atomic force microscopy (AFM) image for a typical nanoparticle sample (NP-R2), which displays that the great mass of the nanoparticles were discrete spheres with ca. 55nm of diameter. It was close to that determined by DLS (Table 1). In addition, it can be seen from TEM image that the nanoparticles are almost spherical in shape with an averaged diameter of 62nm (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of pH in buffer solutions
ce:section.ce:para: It has been well documented that fluorescein undergoes structural reciprocation with the variation of pH value [23]. As demonstrated in Fig. S4, the structural conversion from monoanion state at acidic pH to dianion state at neutral or alkaline pH causes significant increase in its absorption intensity at around 490nm. In addition, the absorption cross section of FITC at pH 2.0 or 8.0 can be determined according to the Beer-Lambert Law. As displayed in Fig. S5, the absorption cross section of FITC at pH 8.0 is 45 times greater than that at pH 2.0, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity at pH 8.0 is 120 times larger than that at pH 2.0 (Fig. S5). That is to say, the size of the absorption cross section directly affects the fluorescence intensity of the dyes. Fig. S6 presents the change of the spectral overlap between the emission band of EANI (donor) and the absorption band of fluorescein moieties at pH 2.0 and pH 8.0. Moreover, the fluorescence emission spectrum of nanoparticles contained only EANI (NP-E1) in different pH solutions have also been recorded, as shown in Fig. S7. It is clear that the emission band of NP-E1 has no obvious change with different pH values, indicating that EANI-FITC pair coupled onto the nanoparticles can be used as the FRET-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for pH detection.

Fig. 2 shows the pH-responsive properties of the dual fluorescent nanoparticles. By varying the feeding ratios of the donor-acceptor during the synthesis process, three kinds of nanoparticles with different donor-acceptor ratios were fabricated. As displays in Fig. 2A–C, the weight ratios of EANI and FITC were 0.2, 0.3 and 0.4, respectively. It can be seen that with the pH increasing from 2.0 to 8.0, the emission of EANI decreased while the emission of FITC is distinctly improved. While the FRET could hardly take place at pH 2.0 due to the minor spectral overlap between the donor and acceptor. When the pH increase to 8.0, the resultant fluorescein moieties exhibit strong absorbance, resulting in stronger energy transfer via increased spectral overlap, clearly indicates that the FRET process is activated by increasing pH in this system.

Fig. 2D shows the change in ratiometric fluorescence intensity at 432nm and 528nm. The on/off ratios were estimated by I(pH 2.0)/I(pH 8.0) at 432nm that is a maximum emission peaks of EANI. As for the FITC, the on/off ratios were estimated by I(pH 8.0)/I(pH 2.0) at 528nm. As the weight ratios of EANI and FITC (mEANI/mFITC) was increased from 0.2 to 0.4, the on/off ratio at 432nm (I(pH 2.0)/I(pH 8.0)) slightly increased, while the on/off ratio at 528nm (I(pH 8.0)/I(pH 2.0)) gradually decreased. It should be considered that the number of FITC residing around one EANI gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of EANI (Table 1), thus fluorescence on/off ratio (528nm) steadily decreased. Consideration of the contrast of detection and the FRET efficiency, we chose nanoparticle with a weight ratio (mEANI/mFITC) of 0.3 to carry out the following experiments.

To further study energy transfer from EANI to FITC, fluorescence images and fluorescence lifetimes has been recorded, as revealed in Fig. 3 . The RFPNS solution emitted intense blue fluorescence when the pH is 2.0, while it turned into luminous green when the pH was 8.0. Therefore, this phenomenon waspreliminarilyapproved and applied for estimating pH value change in organism physiological process. On the other hand, the lifetimes of the donor (EANI) in RFPNS are also obtained from the decay curves at pH 8.0 and pH 2.0, as given in Fig.3B. The lifetime of EANI decreased from 7.4 to 5.5ns when the pH increased from 2.0 to 8.0. The change lifetime of the donor directly certifies the occurrence of FRET process by increasing pH.

It has been well documented that the FRET efficiency (E) may be influenced by some factors, such as the donor-acceptor distance and the energy level match [44,45]. In this work, we prepared several fluorescent nanoparticle samples with varied amounts of donor (EANI) while the fixed amounts of acceptor (FITC), and obtained some of their structural and spectral parameters, as listed in Table 2 . According to the Förster nonradiative energy transfer theory [5], the energy transfer efficiency E, expressed by Eq. (1), depends on the Förster critical radius R 0 and the distance (r) between the donor (EANI) in the PMMA core and the acceptor (FITC) on the particle surface. The energy transfer is effective over distances in the R 0 =±50% R 0 range [5].
(1)
E =
R
0
6
R
0
6
+
r
6

With the data listed in Table 2, we can gain some insight into the effect of the amount of donor on the FRET efficiency (equal to E) and the donor-acceptor distance (r). As we can see in Table 2, for the nanoparticle sample with smallest amount of EANI (NP-R1), the fluorescent intensity of it can be quenched by FITC, the energy transfer efficiency E is 49.4% at pH 8.0 (Fig.2A and Table 2), and the r is 4.5nm. By contrast, for the nanoparticle samples with larger amount of EANI (NP-R2), its E reached a higher value (55.0%) at pH 8.0, and the r is smaller (4.4nm). However, the further increase of EANI (NP-R3) did not show any obvious promotion of the energy transfer efficiency (57.2%). Thus, to ensure high FRET efficiency, the appropriate amount of EANI should be taken.

Subsequently, the fluorescence emission spectrum changes of RFPNS (NP-R2, 0.78mg/mL) were carried out in different pH buffer solutions, as depicted in Fig.4 A. Under different pH, the EANI emission gently decreased at 432nm, and the emission of fluorescein appeared at 528nm, suggesting that the fluorescein moiety from monoanion state to dianion state was triggered by increasing pH. In addition, an excellent linear fitting curve with the correlation coefficient, R2 =0.9909, by plotting I528/I432 versus the logarithm of pH value (3.0–8.0) was also established (Fig.4B), which allowing the accurate measurement of pH in biological or environmental condition. From the figure we can see that the ratio (I528/I432) increased steadily with the increasing pH, which provided additional evidence that pH indeed induced the remarkable change of fluorescence signal at 432nm and 528nm.

Moreover, the effect of ionic concentration (0.01, 0.05, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25 and 0.30mol/L KCl) on the fluorescence intensity ratio(I528/I432) of RFPNS (NP-R2) was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S8, no obvious change of I528/I432 for RFPNS was seen when treated with the KCl at relatively high concentration (up to 0.30mol/L), revealing the proton recognition ability of RFPNS wasn’t interfered by ionic concentration (KCl). On the other hand, the RFPNS shows also good reversibility (six cycles) between pH 3.0 and 8.0 (Fig. S9), making it as a promising ratiometric fluorescence sensor for measurement of intracellular pH values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lysosome-targetable fluorescence imaging in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: The prominent spectral features of RFPNS prompted us to test its potential applications for monitoring lysosomal pH changes in living cells. MTT assays were carried out firstly to evaluate the cytotoxicity of RFPNS to HeLa cells prior to apply the RFPNS in cellular imaging. As displayed in Fig. S10, with 24h incubation, there is no obvious decrease of cell viability for cells after treating with the RFPNS even up to 0.1mg/mL, confirming it can be used for cell imaging.

To determine the subcellular locations of RFPNS in the HeLa cells, two commercially available lysosome or mitochondria specific staining probes (Lyso Tracker Red or MitoTracker Red) were selected to co-stain cells with RFPNS. Fig. 5 shows confocal images of the cells co-stained by RFPNS and Lyso Tracker Red or MitoTracker Red. By comparison of the location of the red fluorescence from Lyso Tracker or Mito-Tracker and the blue fluorescence from RFPNS, It is obvious that there is a significant overlap between RFPNS and Lyso Tracker but not Mito-Tracker. Moreover, overlay of blue and related red images (Fig.5C) depicted pink pointed pattern within the cells, indicated that RFPNS is mainly localized in lysosome. It has been well documented that materials with more negatively charged can escape from endosomes, while materials with positively charged remained trapped inside endosomes, and eventually entered into lysosome [46,47]. The zeta potential (ζ-potential) for the as-prepared RFPNS is +44.8±1.25mV (Fig. S11), thus the particles specially accumulated in lysosome. Therefore, the localization result of lysosome confirmed that RFPNS were adopted by the live cells with good cell-membrane permeability and then accumulated in lysosomes.

To investigate the feasibility of intracellular pH sensing of RFPNS, HeLa cells were incubated with RFPNS. After that, the cells were fixed and exposed to different pH buffer solutions treated with acid or NH4Cl, as exhibited in Fig. 6 . As can be seen from Fig. 6, upon treatment with acetic acid [48,49], the blue signal increased and the green signal decreased, when compare to those of the cells in PBS, and the ratio imaging (Igreen/Iblue, pseudo color generated by Image J software) is decreased (Fig.6O and (II)), indicative of a acidification effect inside the cells. In contrast, ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) can be used to induce intracellular alkalization [50]. Stained cells with RFPNS exhibit contrary result with before after addition of 20mM NH4Cl. The corresponding the ratio imaging (Igreen/Iblue, Fig.6E and (II)) is increased, clearly demonstrated the alkalization effect inside the cells. Thus, the RFPNS can be acted as an excellent sensing system for mapping intracellular pH change.

It has been well documented that the pH driven by the V-ATPase proton pump is decreased during maturation of endosomes to lysosomes [51,52]. As a specific inhibitor of V-ATPase, bafilomycin A1 can not only inhibit the acidification of phagosomes, but also accumulate the weak base acridine-orange in acidic compartments [53–55]. To prove that the fluorescence of BRFPNS was lysosomal pH dependent, the cells contained BRFPNS were performed before and after treatment with bafilomycin A1, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . For the stained cells with RFPNS which were treated with bafilomycin A1 (+Baf), the green signal increased and the blue signal decreased in comparison to those from control, and the ratio imaging is increased (Fig.7E), suggesting that it is the treatment with bafilomycin A1 which causing the increase of pH. The above results revealed that RFPNS can be conveniently used to monitor the lysosomal pH changes.

In addition, the comparison of the nanosensor in this work with those of others are listed Table S2. It is noteworthy to point out that the nanosensor herein is operable in 100% aqueous media, and it can be applied to imaging of lysosomal pH changes inside live cells.

In summary, we present a facile miniemulsion polymerization and surface modification technique for preparing the FRET-based ratiometric fluorescent polymer nanoparticle pH sensor. The resulting RFPNS exhibited favorable water dispersibility (100% aqueous media), good pH-induced spectral reversibility (six cycles) and good biocompatibility. Moreover, after ingested by HeLa cells, the as-prepared RFPNS are mainly located in lysosomes; and can successfully visualize the intracellular lysosomal pH changes. Comparing with the previous reports [29–33], the main advantage of the RFPNS is that they are easy and cheap to prepare and do not make use of complex organic syntheses to generate a water-soluble pH-responsive system. On the one hand, this polymer nanoparticle-based chemsensor will be applied for targeting other analytes such as metal ions, anions, and other biomolecules by conveniently changing the fluorophores and probes. On the other hand, this pH-responsive nanosensor offers an alternative choice for future development of pH sensitive drug delivery system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support of the present work by NSFC (Project no. 51373002, 51603067), Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars, Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Project no. 2014M550418, 2015T80867), and Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics (Project no. 2013008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2016.09.009.
